pain_point_label: Financial Stress & Tuition Arrears Leading to Silent Dropouts

matches.match_rationale: The core pain mechanism is silent dropout — families disengage before the school can intervene. Propensity modeling can score each enrolled student's likelihood of dropping out based on arrears history, payment gap duration, attendance decline, and sibling enrollment patterns. This directly addresses the absence of an early warning system for at-risk families described in the pain point.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A dropout-risk scoring model that flags families with arrears crossing a threshold alongside attendance dips, triggering a counselor outreach queue before the family withdraws quietly. Scores updated weekly from the school's SIS and billing data.

matches.match_rationale: Financial stress avoidance means parents don't call the school — they go silent. A conversational agent reachable via SMS or messaging app (Viber/WhatsApp, common in the Philippines) lets families discreetly ask about payment options, request a payment plan, or flag hardship without the shame of a face-to-face office visit. This directly attacks the humiliation barrier that drives silent withdrawal.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A Viber/SMS chatbot that proactively messages families with overdue accounts, explains flexible payment options, and captures a payment commitment or hardship flag — routing confirmed hardship cases to the finance counselor with full conversation context.

matches.match_rationale: Schools lack a proactive communication infrastructure — there are no templated, empathetic outreach messages for arrears families, no flexible payment plan offer letters, and no scripts for finance counselors. Text generation can produce personalized, tone-calibrated messages (in Filipino/English) that treat the family with dignity rather than issuing a cold demand notice, reducing the humiliation factor.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI-assisted communication layer that drafts individualized arrears outreach messages, payment plan proposal letters, and counselor call scripts — adjusting tone based on arrears severity, payment history, and family profile — for staff review before sending.

matches.match_rationale: Not all families in arrears are equally at risk of dropout — some are temporarily late, others are in structural hardship. Classifying families into risk tiers (e.g., temporary cash-flow gap vs. sustained inability to pay vs. willful non-payment) from structured billing, enrollment, and demographic data enables targeted interventions: payment plans vs. emergency aid vs. collections. This addresses the school's inability to triage at-risk families.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A tiered-risk classifier trained on arrears duration, payment cadence, sibling count, grade level, and prior year patterns that segments families into intervention buckets — enabling the finance team to prioritize counselor outreach vs. automated reminders vs. aid referrals.

matches.match_rationale: The school has no systematic process for identifying hidden dropouts (students who stop attending but aren't formally withdrawn) or for triggering intervention workflows when arrears accumulate. A task automation agent can monitor the SIS daily, cross-reference attendance and billing records, flag anomalies, and automatically initiate outreach workflows — closing the operational gap that allows silent dropouts to go undetected for weeks.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated monitoring pipeline that daily reconciles attendance records against enrollment and billing status, flags students absent 5+ days with outstanding arrears, and creates counselor tasks in the school's CRM or task system — no manual monitoring required.
